title: The Applications We've Covered
concept: Phase 10 spanned thirteen distinct real-world computer vision applications, from facial recognition through 3D vision — each building on the CNN, architecture, and training foundations from earlier phases.

exercises.prompt: Recap: Why would a robot gripper favor explicit 3D geometry (like a point cloud) over a NeRF for physical grasping?

options.text: A gripper needs concrete spatial coordinates to plan contact points, which explicit geometry provides directly
NeRFs are always faster for robots to use than point clouds
Grasping requires no spatial representation at all

exercises.explain: Yes! Explicit geometry gives directly usable spatial coordinates.

cards.title: Looking back at the journey
cards.body: Thirteen units ago, we started with a simple question: can a computer tell whether a photo contains a face? Since then, we've covered an enormous range of real-world vision applications built on top of the CNN foundations from earlier phases.

cards.title: Phase 10 Recap: Advanced Vision Applications
cards.body: We covered: face detection/recognition & pose estimation (Units 163-165), OCR & image captioning (166-167), neural style transfer (168), super-resolution (169), multi-object tracking (170), image retrieval (171), anomaly detection (172), depth estimation (173), and 3D vision — point clouds and NeRFs (174).

cards.title: A specialist's full toolbox
cards.body: Earlier phases built the CNN 'engine' — convolutions, architectures, training tricks. Phase 10 was about filling a toolbox with SPECIALIZED tools built on top of that engine: a face-finder, a text-reader, an art-styler, a tracker, a search engine, a defect-spotter, a distance-ruler, and a 3D-scanner.

cards.title: A smart city combining several tools
cards.body: A smart city traffic system might combine several Phase 10 tools at once: object detection + tracking to follow vehicles, OCR to read license plates, anomaly detection to spot stalled cars, and depth estimation to judge following distances — all working together.

cards.title: These applications aren't isolated silos
cards.body: It's easy to think of each unit as a totally separate, unrelated tool. In practice, real systems often COMBINE multiple applications together — a security camera might use face detection, tracking, AND anomaly detection simultaneously, all built on the same underlying CNN/Transformer backbone principles.

cards.title: Foundational Phases vs. Phase 10 Applications
cards.body: How this phase built on everything before it:

left.text:
Convolutions, architectures, training
Classification, detection, segmentation
The general-purpose CNN toolkit

right.text:
Specialized real-world tools
Built directly on the CNN toolkit
Faces, text, tracking, 3D, and more

exercises.prompt: What was the central theme connecting all of Phase 10's units?

options.text: Specialized real-world computer vision applications built on top of the CNN foundations from earlier phases

exercises.explain: Correct! Phase 10 applied earlier CNN foundations to specialized real-world tasks.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Real-world systems typically use only ONE Phase 10 application in complete isolation, never combining several together.
exercises.explain: False. Real systems often combine multiple applications together, like a security system using detection, tracking, and anomaly detection simultaneously.

exercises.sentence: Phase 10's specialized applications were all built on top of the CNN ___ established in earlier phases.
exercises.explain: Earlier phases built the general CNN toolkit that Phase 10's applications specialize.

exercises.prompt: Why might a smart city traffic system combine object tracking, OCR, and anomaly detection together rather than using just one technique alone?

options.text: Different techniques solve different sub-problems (following vehicles, reading plates, spotting stalled cars) that together create a complete traffic monitoring system
Combining techniques is always unnecessary and adds no value
Only one vision technique can ever run on a single camera feed

exercises.explain: Correct! Combining specialized tools creates more capable, complete real-world systems.

exercises.prompt: A hospital wants a system that both identifies patients at the door (face recognition) AND flags unusual equipment behavior (anomaly detection) using the same camera network. What does this best illustrate about Phase 10's applications?

options.text: Multiple specialized Phase 10 techniques can be combined within one real-world system to solve complementary sub-problems
Only one Phase 10 technique can ever be deployed per organization
Face recognition and anomaly detection are actually the exact same technique
Hospitals cannot use any computer vision techniques for safety reasons

exercises.explain: Exactly! Real systems compose multiple specialized tools together. Next: how to actually choose the right application architecture for a given problem.
